review_summary: This unit supports procedural questions about the skip-gram training setup, context windows, word types versus tokens, and likelihood objectives.

question_bank.question: In the skip-gram-style word2vec setup, what is the model trained to predict?
choices: Nearby outside words given a center word within a context window.
The next character in a word given its spelling.
The dictionary definition of every word type.
The document title given the final sentence.
question_bank.explanation: The training signal comes from local co-occurrence. For each center word, the model adjusts vectors so observed nearby words receive higher probability.

question_bank.question: What is the difference between a word type and a word token in this setup?
choices: A type is the vocabulary item; a token is a particular occurrence of that item in text.
A type is a context window; a token is the learned objective function.
A type is always rare; a token is always frequent.
A type is a dense vector; a token is a one-hot vector only.
question_bank.explanation: The model learns a vector for a word as a vocabulary item, while individual appearances of that word in the corpus are tokens used during training.

question_bank.question: Why does the word2vec objective use negative log likelihood rather than raw likelihood?
choices: It turns maximizing observed context probability into a minimization objective that is easier to optimize.
It removes the need to use probability distributions over context words.
It ensures unrelated words always get probability exactly zero.
It converts every dense vector back into a one-hot vector.
question_bank.explanation: Optimization is commonly framed as minimizing a loss. Taking the negative log of likelihood preserves the goal of assigning high probability to observed contexts while producing a convenient loss.

question_bank.question: Which example best illustrates the intended behavior of the word2vec training objective?
choices: Assigning high probability to a plausible neighbor such as “crises” near “banking.”
Assigning equal probability to every vocabulary item near every center word.
Ignoring nearby words and using only document-level labels.
Replacing every word with its WordNet synonym before training.
question_bank.explanation: The objective rewards vectors that make real neighboring words likely. A plausible context word should score higher than an unrelated word in that local window.
